proposed_answer: A minimalist version connects the ankles and wrists, while a more structured version can attach the legs to a chest harness or box tie.
proposed_question: How does a minimalist hogtie differ from a more structured version?
reason: This contrasts the two explicitly described constructions without adding hidden video steps or recommending either version.
support_evidence:
| Day 36: Ankles to Wrists – Make a minimalist hogtie by tying the ankles and wrists together. | 
| Day 37: Ankles to Chest – Add a bit more structure by using a chest harness or a box tie as an anchor to attach the legs. |

proposed_answer: Bodies are not perfectly symmetric, and asymmetric options can accommodate side-to-side differences or injuries.
proposed_question: Why can asymmetric arm ties be useful for adapting to a person?
reason: This preserves the source's body-variation and injury-adaptation rationale without suggesting a tie is automatically safe for an injury.
support_evidence: Beautiful shapes emerge out of imbalance. Pulling the wrists in different directions will pose the arms in an infinite number of combinations. It is interesting to observe how each side can be different as well. Our bodies aren’t completely symmetric. Differences can be big or small, and it becomes really important to have asymmetric ties in our toolbox to adapt when injuries happen.

proposed_answer: Place a block or cushion under the seated person.
proposed_question: How can hero pose be modified when tying someone who finds the position difficult?
reason: This is the source's explicit flexibility modification in a short standalone answer.
support_evidence: Day 143: Hero Pose – Alt. petanko-zuwari ぺたんこ座り (flat sitting). Tie the feet on each side in a kneeling position. This position may be difficult depending on flexibility. Sit on a block or a cushion to make it easier.
